en.extract:
The sandy scops owl (Otus icterorhynchus), or cinnamon scops owl, is an owl from the family Strigidae found in Africa.


Taxonomy
The sandy scops owl is thought to be basal in the lineage of the genus Otus, along with the Sokoke scops owl (Otus ireneae), but there has been no genetic material for testing from the sandy scops owl. In addition, some authorities consider that the subspecies of sandy scops owl should be treated as two different species.


Subspecies
Two subspecies are currently recognised:

Otus icterorhynchus icterorhynchus: West Africa
Otus icterorhynchus holerythrus: northwestern central Africa.


Description
A small owl which has an overall pale rufous colour, a plain face, a pale yellow bill and yellow eyes. The pale stripe along the shoulders is quite obvious. The adults are pale rufous brown, finely spotted with white and barred with buff. The underparts are paler and more rufous with buff bars and white spots on the belly and vent. The tail, flight feathers and greater upperwing coverts are rufous barred with black. Length is about 15 cm (5.9 in), and wingspan is 45 cm (18 in).


Voice
The sound is a long whistling "twooo" which descends in pitch.


Distribution and habitat
The sandy scops owl has a scattered distribution across the African tropical rainforest ; it has been recorded from Guinea, Liberia, Ivory Coast, Ghana, Cameroon, Gabon, Congo, Democratic Republic of Congo and  Central African Republic.
The sandy scops owl occurs in lowland evergreen forest, open canopy forest and scrub, or in open forest mosaic land.


Behaviour
The sandy scops owl is rarely seen and little is known about its habits. It is probably crepuscular and insectivorous feeding largely  on grasshoppers and crickets. The breeding season is probably during February and March.


References

BirdLife Species Factsheet
Article title

The '''sandy scops owl''' ('''''Otus icterorhynchus'''''), or '''cinnamon scops owl''', is an owl from the family [[Strigidae]] found in [[Africa]].

==Taxonomy==
The sandy scops owl is thought to be basal in the lineage of the genus ''Otus'', along with the [[Sokoke scops owl]] (''Otus ireneae''), but there has been no genetic material for testing from the sandy scops owl.<ref name = Fuchs>{{cite journal | last1 = Fuchs| first1= Jérôme |display-authors=etal | year = 2008 | title = Tracing the colonization history of the Indian Ocean scops-owls (Strigiformes: Otus) with further insight into the spatio-temporal origin of the Malagasy avifauna | doi = 10.1186/1471-2148-8-197 | pmid= 18611281 | journal = BMC Evolutionary Biology | volume=8 | page=197| pmc = 2483963 | doi-access= free }}</ref> In addition, some authorities consider that the subspecies of sandy scops owl should be treated as two different species.<ref name = HBW>{{cite web | url = http://www.hbw.com/species/sandy-scops-owl-otus-icterorhynchus | title = Sandy Scops-owl (''Otus icterorhynchus'') | access-date = 28 October 2016 | publisher = Lynx Edicions|work = Handbook of the birds of the World Alive}}</ref>

==Subspecies==
Two subspecies are currently recognised:<ref name = Avibase>{{cite web | url = http://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=9CD0C8FE4C03278E | title = Sandy Scops-Owl ''Otus icterorhynchus'' (Shelley, 1873) | access-date = 28 October 2016 | publisher = Denis Lepage|work = Avibase}}</ref>
*''Otus icterorhynchus icterorhynchus'': West Africa
*''Otus icterorhynchus holerythrus'': northwestern central Africa.

==Description==
A small owl which has an overall pale rufous colour, a plain face, a pale yellow bill and yellow eyes. The pale stripe along the shoulders is quite obvious. The adults are pale rufous brown, finely spotted with white and barred with buff. The underparts are paler and more rufous with buff bars and white spots on the belly and vent. The tail, flight feathers and greater upperwing coverts are rufous barred with black. Length is about {{convert|15|cm|abbr=on}}, and wingspan is {{cvt|45|cm|abbr=on}}.<ref name = Kemp>{{cite book |last1= Kemp |first1= Alan |last2= Kemp |first2= Meg |year= 1998 |title= Birds of Prey of Africa and its Islands |pages= 314–315 |publisher= New Holland |isbn= 1-85974-100-2}}</ref>

===Voice===
The sound is a long whistling "twooo" which descends in pitch.<ref name = Kemp/>

==Distribution and habitat==
The sandy scops owl has a scattered distribution across the [[Tropical forest|African tropical rainforest]] ; it has been recorded from [[Guinea]], [[Liberia]], [[Ivory Coast]], [[Ghana]], [[Cameroon]], [[Gabon]], [[republic of Congo|Congo]], [[Democratic Republic of Congo]] and  [[Central African Republic]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

The sandy scops owl occurs in lowland evergreen forest, open canopy forest and scrub, or in open forest mosaic land.<ref name = Avibirds.Owls>{{cite web | url = http://www.avibirds.com/html/Owls/Sandy_Scops_Owl.html#.WBM--aPIYyI | title = Profile Sandy Scops Owl |access-date= 28 October 2016 |publisher= Avibirds |work= Avibirds.Owls}}</ref>

==Behaviour== 
The sandy scops owl is rarely seen and little is known about its habits. It is probably crepuscular and insectivorous<ref name = Avibirds.Owls/> feeding largely  on grasshoppers and crickets. The breeding season is probably during February and March.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />
